title: What counts as a view?
learningObjectives: Explain what counts as a view? in plain language
Write down the objective and identify its assumptions
Design one test that could falsify an over-strong interpretation

whyThisLesson.text: A view is a second observation of the same underlying item that changes some information while preserving something you want to identify. This lesson gives you a concrete test for that claim before the course moves to the next abstraction.

intuition.analogy: Two cameras can film the same event from different angles. What both cameras see is shared; their lighting, occlusion, and viewpoint are private. A view is a second observation of the same underlying item that changes some information while preserving something you want to identify.
intuition.analogyLimit: Correlation identifies agreement, not necessarily cause. A shared nuisance can look just as attractive as a shared concept.
intuition.text: A view is a second observation of the same underlying item that changes some information while preserving something you want to identify.

toyExample.description: Create two views with one shared factor and one private factor. Increase private signal and watch an alignment-only objective ignore it.

steps.result: Specify exactly what what counts as a view? should recover, preserve, or ignore.
steps.insight: The intended factor must be known before a toy result can be judged.

steps.result: Use the same data, capacity, training budget, and evaluation examples for both methods.
steps.insight: Otherwise a visible gain may be a resource difference in disguise.

steps.result: Vary the source correlation, noise, view quality, or sparsity and record the first failure.
steps.insight: The boundary of success is usually more useful than the headline score.

visual.question: Which assumption behind what counts as a view? changes the result most?
visual.description: Move one control at a time. Treat the display as a toy model, then write down the real experiment it suggests.

technical.text: A view is a second observation of the same underlying item that changes some information while preserving something you want to identify. In practice, write down the input unit, the operation applied to it, and the exact statistic used to score the result. Those are different things. A learned representation can be useful even when the interpretation attached to it turns out to be wrong.

mathematics.explanation: For this lesson, the expression makes the central trade-off explicit. A view is a second observation of the same underlying item that changes some information while preserving something you want to identify.
mathematics.whyNeeded: It says precisely what the method is being rewarded for.
mathematics.ifRemoved: You could not tell whether two implementations were solving the same problem.
mathematics.encourages: A testable prediction about the chosen objective.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: Correlation identifies agreement, not necessarily cause. A shared nuisance can look just as attractive as a shared concept.

methodComparison.question: What information is rewarded?
methodComparison.practicalImplication: Alignment alone can discard valuable private signal.

evidence.claim: A view is a second observation of the same underlying item that changes some information while preserving something you want to identify.
evidence.note: Course explanation. Use the research library's primary sources for paper-specific empirical claims.

failureModes.name: A score that answers the wrong question
failureModes.mechanism: The objective rewards a proxy while the paper describes a stronger scientific property.
failureModes.symptom: The headline metric rises, but seed stability, controls, or interventions disagree.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Add a matched negative control and one counterfactual intervention.
failureModes.mitigation: Either narrow the claim or add the missing measurement.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: Report both the intended benefit and the most plausible shortcut for every SetConCA loss term.

setconcaConnection.relevance: What counts as a view? determines how SetConCA should represent, group, or evaluate activation views.
setconcaConnection.whichPart: The choice of sparse code, view set, coordination loss, or evaluation.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: Match the pointwise SAE and SetConCA on model, activation site, width, sparsity budget, and compute.
setconcaConnection.whichFailure: Mistaking stronger agreement for recovery of the intended concept.
setconcaConnection.whichExperiment: Compare fidelity, feature stability, selectivity, and controlled interventions; report each result separately.
setconcaConnection.alternativeExplanation: An apparent gain may come from extra context, effective capacity, or easier positives rather than set coordination.

exercises.prompt: A paper claims that what counts as a view? improved interpretability. What one result would you ask for before accepting that claim?
exercises.reveal: Ask for the smallest missing control: a matched baseline, several seeds, a negative control, or an intervention that tests the claimed mechanism.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Explain what counts as a view? to a colleague, then name one thing the method cannot establish by itself.

nextLesson.bridge: Next: CCA: align shared variation. The next idea reuses this lesson's representation and makes a new assumption explicit.

fields.label: Falsifiable claim
fields.prompt: Write the smallest claim this lesson supports.

fields.label: Control
fields.prompt: What control would rule out a shortcut?
